rows.v: Unaged, or aged then charcoal-filtered for clarity
Sugarcane, light citrus, coconut, subtle vanilla
Ceviche, grilled shrimp, tropical fruit
Mixing — the cleanest canvas for citrus-forward cocktails
Lightly aged in oak, typically 1–4 years
Vanilla, caramel, toasted almond, light oak
Grilled pork, baked plantain, pecan desserts
A step up from white rum — sippable on its own or in slightly richer cocktails
Longer aging, often 5+ years, sometimes blended with rich molasses-based rums
Molasses, baking spice, dark chocolate, dried fruit
BBQ, gingerbread, dark chocolate desserts
Often the most intensely flavored category — a little goes a long way in cocktails, which is why it's usually "floated" rather than fully mixed.
Varies — base rum infused with spices after aging
Cinnamon, nutmeg, vanilla, clove, sometimes vanilla bean
Spiced cake, apple desserts, holiday cooking
Closer to a flavored liqueur than a "pure" rum category — great with cola, less great in classic rum cocktails that expect a clean base.
White rum, fresh lime juice, simple syrup
Crisp
Refreshing
Balanced
The "real" Daiquiri is just these three ingredients, shaken — not the frozen, fruity slushy version. Ask for it "classic" if you want the original.
White rum, mint, lime, sugar, soda water
Herby
Effervescent
Lively
The mint should be gently pressed, not pulverized — over-muddled mint turns bitter and grassy.
Dark rum (floated), ginger beer, lime juice
Spicy
Deep
Robust
The dark rum should sit on top, not be stirred in — that visual "storm cloud" layering is part of the drink.
Rum blend (white + dark), orgeat, lime juice, orange liqueur
Tropical
Complex
Nutty
Orgeat (almond syrup) is the key ingredient that separates a real Mai Tai from a sugary imitation — a good bar will have it on hand.
White or gold rum, pineapple juice, cream of coconut
Creamy
Sweet
Indulgent
A gold rum float on top adds depth without changing the creamy base — ask for it if you want a slightly more grown-up version.
